reasoning_summary: The live run context for `20260421_155210_bf16_gemm_v1_9cac32cb` at 24.346112 ms says round 6 succeeded as a recovery step but not as a new base. Restoring the 128x128 PTX family and batching export tiles cut runtime by 5.822463 ms versus the losing 256x128 branch, recovered tensor activity to 48.38%, and pulled barrier down from 8.32% to 5.61%. But it still missed the accepted non-PTX base at 24.195072 ms by about 0.151 ms, while shared memory rose to 26,112 B and registers returned to 200. That means the PTX-local surgery paid back most of the regression but did not become the next winning surface. The ranking should now move to the cleanest alternate family on top of the accepted base: restore the non-PTX 128x128 sibling and probe whether a 3-CTA residency target can improve latency hiding there without the PTX export tax. Keep one tighter PTX wait/sync-collapse fallback alive because the current run still shows 5.61% barrier and 7.09% long scoreboard, and keep the 256x128 half-panel family in third place because the human guidance still points there even though the latest two clean-loop probes did not justify another immediate 256x128 implementation.
family_audit: Tiling: keep the 64x64 warp-tile idea accepted, but defer another immediate 256x128 block-tile edit. Round 4 and round 5 already showed that the current correctness-safe 256x128 surface is not ready to become the next baseline.
Coalescing Access: keep as a guardrail only. The recovered PTX run already restored memory and tensor throughput materially, so the next ranking is not blocked on a clear coalescing miss.

Data Reuse: accept as existing baseline behavior. Shared-memory reuse is already present on both 128x128 and 256x128 families, so reuse alone does not explain the remaining 0.151 ms miss versus the accepted base.
Async Copy: accept strongly. The current evidence says the copy path itself is not missing; the remaining issue is how the staged tiles are handed off and consumed.
Bank Conflict: defer again. The recovered run is still dominated by barrier and long-scoreboard cost rather than a new shared-bank signature.
L2 Cache: defer. Round 6 already restored L2 and memory throughput well enough that cache movement is not the next priority.

Register Reuse: accept, but keep it split across two families. Use the non-PTX 128x128 sibling as the next bounded probe, and keep the deeper 256x128 half-panel reuse branch queued instead of spending consecutive rounds there.
Pg2s: accept as infrastructure already in place. The ranking question is no longer whether global-to-shared prefetch exists, but which family uses it with the least synchronization tax.
Ps2r: accept strongly. The PTX run showed that export and fragment-lifetime control still matter, so one PTX-local wait/sync-collapse fallback stays in the queue.
Stage: accept, but reject further shared-footprint growth on the PTX path. Round 6 paid back performance but still lost to the accepted base after raising shared memory to 26,112 B.
Promote the non-PTX 128x128 sibling occupancy probe to rank 1 because it is now the cleanest alternate family on top of the accepted base rather than another repair-only step.

Keep a tighter PTX wait/sync-collapse branch at rank 2 because round 6 showed the PTX family still has actionable barrier and scoreboard slack, but it should not stay rank 1 after missing the accepted base.
Keep the 256x128 half-panel family at rank 3 because the human tiling guidance still matters, but the current clean loop does not justify reopening it immediately.

directions.name: Force 3-CTA Residency On The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling
directions.hypothesis: Round 6 proved that recovering from the losing 256x128 branch was the right call, but it also showed that the PTX-local export batching variant is still not the best 128x128 surface. The accepted base remains the non-PTX 128x128 sibling at 24.195072 ms. The cleanest next probe is therefore to restore that sibling and test whether a 3-CTA launch-bounds target can improve latency hiding there without paying the PTX microkernel export tax that kept round 6 slightly above the accepted base.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-limited occupancy and latency hiding on the accepted non-PTX 128x128 hot-band surface.
directions.risk: Moderate. The restore surface is correctness-safe and already faster than the current run, but the 3-CTA budget can still trade registers for spills or barrier regressions.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: This is now the cleanest alternate-family test because it starts from the accepted base instead of trying to repair the still-slower PTX branch again.
It keeps the search moving without reopening the already-failed 256x128 surface immediately.
It also preserves the human guidance around reuse, staging, and structured tiling while switching to the lower-friction control path.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if registers fail to fall materially, or if active warps improve but barrier cost rises enough to leave runtime above the accepted base again.
expected_local_changes: Restore the accepted non-PTX 128x128 sibling launch path.
Raise the non-PTX sibling launch-bounds target to probe whether 3-CTA residency is viable on the accepted base.

directions.name: Collapse PTX Wait-Group Handoff Without Extra Export Scratch
directions.hypothesis: Round 6 showed that the PTX family still has real slack: tensor activity recovered to 48.38%, barrier dropped to 5.61%, and short scoreboard fell sharply, but the run still missed the accepted base because the export-batching fix also raised shared footprint and left 7.09% long-scoreboard stall on the table. A narrower fallback is to keep the PTX anchor but target the wait-group and refill ordering directly, without adding extra export scratch or repeating the stage-growth pattern.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The seam between cp.async wait-group release, __syncthreads(), and future-stage refill ordering on the PTX 128x128 microkernel.
directions.risk: Moderate-high. This stays on a bounded PTX surface, but it is another control-flow retime and must avoid repeating the shared-footprint growth that already missed the accepted base.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: Keep one PTX-local fallback alive because the recovered run still shows meaningful barrier and long-scoreboard cost.
Rank it second because round 6 proved that another PTX-local fix is now a refinement path, not the best primary branch.
This is also the best remaining path that directly maps to the user's Async Copy, Pg2s, Ps2r, and Stage guidance without reopening 256x128.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the edit keeps shared memory above the accepted-base class or fails to beat the accepted base after reducing barrier or long-scoreboard cost.
expected_local_changes: Keep the PTX 128x128 anchor launch path.
Collapse the wait-group and refill seam without adding more export scratch stages or broader shared-memory growth.

directions.name: Reopen The 256x128 Half-Panel Register-Reuse Branch Later
directions.hypothesis: The 256x128 family should stay in the queue because it is still the family most aligned with the user's explicit tiling guidance and still represents the largest theoretical register-budget upside in repo history. But the current clean loop has not earned another immediate 256x128 edit: the simple pivot promotion regressed badly, and the compact transplant only reproduced the same losing machine state. The right move is to defer this family behind one or two more bounded 128x128 probes.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register reuse, fragment lifetime, and writer-ownership constraints on the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot.
directions.risk: High. This remains the broadest active family and it already consumed two clean-loop rounds without producing a viable surface.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: Keep this family alive because it is still the strongest match to the human 256x128 guidance.
Demote it to third because the current clean loop should not spend three consecutive rounds trying to repair the same still-losing 256x128 surface.
If the next accepted-base probe still plateaus, this remains the obvious broader branch to reopen.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately on another 28-30 ms class regression or on any correctness failure; do not spend consecutive rounds here without a new machine-state change.
expected_local_changes: Keep the correctness-safe 256x128 writer ownership path.
Only reopen this family after the loop has completed another bounded 128x128 probe on or near the accepted base.

notes: Human guidance review for round 7: the latest run validated the choice to leave the losing 256x128 surface, but it did not validate staying on the PTX family as the main branch. The recovered PTX run became a good fallback surface, not the new winner. That is why the ranking now prefers the accepted non-PTX sibling plus one bounded occupancy probe, keeps one PTX wait/sync-collapse refinement in reserve, and leaves the 256x128 family queued rather than deleting it.
